summary: Drew DeVault presents a systematic analysis of Richard Stallman's political writings on sex, consent, and minors, arguing that Stallman's 2019 retraction on pedophilia is misleading due to his idiosyncratic definition of 'children' as only those under 13. The post traces a years-long pattern across Stallman's political notes where he defends adults accused of sexual impropriety with minors, seeks to erode the meaning of 'sexual assault,' and consistently disregards power dynamics in sexual harassment. DeVault argues this is not a series of isolated incidents but a coherent political discourse that normalizes exploitation. He concludes that Stallman's continued leadership role poisons the Free Software movement's culture and that the FSF's failure to act has a chilling effect on the community.
key_points: Stallman's 2019 retraction on pedophilia is effectively meaningless because his personal definition of 'children' excludes anyone over 12-13, leaving his defenses of adult-minor sexual contact with teenagers intact
Stallman has repeatedly used his political platform to defend specific individuals accused of sexual misconduct with minors, including Roy Moore, Cody Wilson, Marvin Minsky, and Jelani Maraj
Stallman's campaign against the term 'sexual assault' serves to downplay crimes ranging from groping to sex with minors, consistently benefiting accused perpetrators
Stallman demonstrates a fundamental blindness to power dynamics in sexual harassment and abuse, whether between adults and minors, managers and subordinates, or public figures and their audience
The Free Software community's continued elevation of Stallman as an ideological leader creates a toxic environment that alienates women and children and risks normalizing exploitation
money_quotes: Stallman appears to have little to no understanding of the role of power dynamics in sexual harassment, assault, and rape.
The failure of the Free Software community to account for Richard Stallman's behavior has a chilling effect.
The norms Stallman endorses are harmful and deeply confronting and alienating to many people, in particular women and children.
One's political qualifications to speak about free software does not make one qualified to address matters of sex; in this respect Stallman's persistence presents as dangerous incompetence.
When we consider his speech on sex as a discourse that has been crafted and rehearsed methodically over the years, he asks us to consider him seriously, and so we must.
The free software movement is culturally poisoned by our support of Stallman as our ideological leader.
It is important to acknowledge this behavior not as a series of isolated incidents, nor a conflict with Stallman's 'personal style', but a pattern of behavior from which a political narrative forms.
key_insight: Stallman's writings on sex constitute not a series of gaffes but a deliberate, years-long political discourse that systematically erodes norms around consent and minor protection, and the Free Software community's refusal to reckon with this poisons its own culture.
